intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to Rust upgrades an application to a compiled systems language that guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector. Developers make this change to eliminate runtime overhead, optimize CPU-bound tasks, and prevent memory leaks in critical microservices. The migration requires swapping high-level runtime flexibility for a strict compile-time borrow checker and explicit variable ownership contracts.

diffs.from: Compile-time structural type matching that supports complex unions and mapped types, erased during build passes.
diffs.to: Strict nominal static typing with comprehensive generic traits, algebraic data types (enums), and compile-time lifetime annotations.

diffs.from: C-style syntax featuring explicit class components, arrow parameters, and structural object configurations.
diffs.to: Expression-oriented block syntax leveraging pattern matching rules, macros, and explicit error propagation operators.

diffs.from: Automated engine heap tracking utilizing runtime generational garbage collection passes.
diffs.to: Compile-time ownership semantics where resources are automatically reclaimed the moment their tracking variable scope ends.

diffs.from: Single-threaded execution loop coordinating micro-tasks, Promise objects, and async/await blocks.
diffs.to: Zero-overhead pull-based Future state machines requiring an external executor runtime like Tokio for multi-threaded polling.

diffs.from: Basic language definitions requiring external npm packages for core filesystem or system operations.
diffs.to: Lean system library delivering high-performance primitive wrappers, collections, and explicit I/O traits.

examples.title: Algebraic Types and Comprehensive Matching
examples.description: Modeling data structures using tagged enums with pattern validation matches.

examples.title: Safe Result Compilation Wrappers
examples.description: Orchestrating robust data pipelines without throw statements.

tips.title: Internalize Ownership and Borrowing Rules
tips.body: Rust allows only one active owner per data resource. When passing variables into functions, the resource is 'moved' unless you pass a reference using an ampersand (`&` for read-only, `&mut` for mutable access).

tips.title: Replace Null States with Options
tips.body: Rust has no null or undefined references. Missing data or partial operations must be modeled explicitly using the `Option<T>` enum, forcing you to handle both `Some(val)` and `None` branches at compile time.

tips.title: Propagate Errors with the Question Mark Operator
tips.body: Instead of throwing exceptions that must be caught with try/catch blocks, return a `Result<T, E>` from functions and append the `?` operator to operations to cleanly propagate errors up the call stack.

tips.title: Opt for Iterators Over Standard Loops
tips.body: Rust collections expose high-performance iterators via `.iter()`. Use functional combinators like `.filter()` and `.map()` directly; the compiler optimizes these into zero-cost loops that perform as fast as manual index updates.
